core_claim: DirtyDecrypt (CVE-2026-31635) is an LPE in rxgk_decrypt_skb missing copy-on-write guard. Impacts only distros with CONFIG_RXGK enabled (Fedora, Arch, openSUSE). Discovered by Zellic/V12, reported as duplicate of already-patched bug. Silent patch meant no coordintated disclosure.

slides.on_slide_copy: They fixed it silently, then dismissed the report.

caption:
DirtyDecrypt (CVE-2026-31635) is an LPE in rxgk_decrypt_skb missing a copy-on-write guard. Only hits distros with CONFIG_RXGK enabled: Fedora, Arch Linux, openSUSE Tumbleweed.

Zellic and V12 reported it May 9. Maintainers said: duplicate, already patched in mainline. No CVE. No advisory. No coordinated disclosure. Researchers forced public when they realized the fix was silent.

The bug: rxgk_decrypt_skb decrypts incoming socket buffer on receive. Memory pages shared with page cache of other processes. Copy-on-write should protect shared pages. The guard was missing. Data writes to privileged process memory or page cache of /etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers, SUID binaries.

This mirrors the Dirty Frag pattern: Hyunwoo Kim forced into public disclosure when embargo broke after merged patch. Independent researcher analyzed public commit and published. Standard n-day weaponization.

Silent patches without CVEs leave defenders blind. Researchers who report responsibly get duplicate responses.

Kernel developers now reviewing emergency killswitch: disable vulnerable function at runtime until real patch arrives. Rocky Linux debuted opt-in security repository for accelerated fixes.

A silent patch is not a fix for defenders who do not know it exists. The killswitch and security repos are symptoms of a disclosure process that leaves gaps.

Do you track silent kernel patches in your distro, or only CVEs?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[DirtyDecrypt, CVE-2026-31635, Linux kernel, rxgk, copy-on-write, silent patch, coordinated disclosure, Zellic, V12, kernel killswitch, Rocky Linux security repo]
comment_prompt: Do you track silent kernel patches in your distro, or only CVEs?
alt_text: Title card: silent patch, no disclosure, researchers told it was a duplicate. DirtyDecrypt, CVE-2026-31635, an rxgk missing copy-on-write guard that only hits Fedora, Arch, and openSUSE, with a PoC released anyway. A small sealed packet of light in a dark void, one freshly added clasp glowing faint green while a faint marking beside it dissolves into gray.

Zellic and V12 reported DirtyDecrypt May 9 and maintainers replied it was a duplicate already patched in mainline: no CVE, no advisory, no coordinated disclosure. Researchers went public on realizing the fix was silent, releasing a PoC, and NVD later linked CVE-2026-31635. Two opposing shapes of light face off in the dark, one reaching out while the other stays cold and turned away.

Only distros with CONFIG_RXGK enabled are affected: Fedora, Arch Linux, and openSUSE Tumbleweed, and in containers a vulnerable worker node can give a pod escape path. CVSS 7.5: the missing guard in rxgk_decrypt_skb writes to /etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers, and SUID binaries. Three identical emblems of light glow hot red while three others nearby stay dark and dormant.

rxgk_decrypt_skb decrypts an incoming sk_buff whose pages are shared with other processes' page cache, where copy-on-write should protect shared pages but the COW guard was missing. Data lands in privileged memory or files, leading to LPE. A translucent buffer of light is unsealed in place, a missing protective shell where a duplicate should have formed, its freed glow bleeding into a nearby locked vault.

Hyunwoo Kim, the Dirty Frag researcher, was forced public when the embargo broke after the patch merged, and another researcher independently analyzed the public commit and published, a standard n-day weaponization. Silent patches without CVEs leave defenders blind. A dark horizontal track runs from a quiet sealed mark, to a dismissed report fading out, to an independent spark, to a forced burst of red light.

Kernel developers are reviewing an emergency killswitch that disables a vulnerable function at runtime, returning an operator-supplied value on every CPU until disengage or reboot. Rocky Linux debuted an opt-in security repository for accelerated fixes. A split view of a mechanism running unchecked and overheating red beside the same mechanism with a kill lever thrown and held safe.

A silent patch is not a fix for defenders who do not know it exists. The kernel killswitch and security repos are symptoms of a disclosure process that leaves gaps. A single freshly sealed clasp glows green while the faint marking that should explain it fades to invisible gray.

Closing question: do you track silent kernel patches in your distro, or only CVEs? Save this for your next vulnerability management review. A single faint pulse of light on a clean dark end-card with open negative space.

narration.text: Here is how a Linux kernel bug got patched without anyone outside the maintainers knowing it was serious.
When Zellic and V12 reported it on May ninth, the maintainers said it was a duplicate that was already fixed in mainline, so no CVE and no advisory ever went out.
The flaw lives in the kernel's rxgk crypto layer, and it only affects kernels that build with that feature turned on, which means Fedora, Arch, and openSUSE.
The decrypt path wrote straight into memory pages that other processes share, because the copy-on-write step that should clone a page before writing was missing.

That let an attacker corrupt privileged files like the password and sudo configuration, and because the fix shipped quietly, someone read it and built a working exploit before defenders even knew to patch.
Now the kernel team is debating a runtime killswitch that disables a bad function on the fly, and Rocky Linux has opened a security repository so fixes can reach people faster.
The lesson is blunt. A patch that nobody announces does not protect the people who never hear about it, and these emergency tools exist because that gap is real.
So here is my question for you. Do you actually track quiet kernel patches in your distro, or do you only watch the CVE feed?

beats.caption: Silent patch. No disclosure. Duplicate response.
Zellic/V12 reported. Maintainers said duplicate. No CVE.
Only CONFIG_RXGK distros. COW guard missing. Shadow sudoers SUID.
rxgk_decrypt_skb decrypts in-place. Missing COW. Bleeds to page cache.
Dirty Frag pattern. Silent patch. Independent analysis. Forced public.
Kernel killswitch. Rocky Linux security repo. Accelerated fixes.
Silent patch not a fix. Disclosure process leaves gaps.
Track silent patches or only CVEs?

video.subtitle_style: large centered lower-third, high contrast, two lines max
